Entry.vjdbv1.1_description: Common abbreviations of the virus species name.
ENA analysis object Accession.
Antiviral treatment used for this subject, such as zanamavir oseltamivir, amantadine.
Dosage of the treatment taken by the subject. example: 0.05 mg.
Duration of antiviral treatment after onset of clinical symptoms in days.example: 5.
Initiation of antiviral treatment after onset of clinical symptoms in days. example: 2.5.

Completeness of the virus genome sequenceas calculated by CheckV v1.1.1 against the default CheckV database v1.5. For more information about CheckV please see the tool page at: https://bitbucket.org/berkeleylab/checkv/src/master

Method of determining the completeness of the virus genome sequence as calculated by CheckV v1.1.1 against the default CheckV database v1.5. For more information about CheckV please see the tool page at: https://bitbucket.org/berkeleylab/checkv/src/master

Amount of virus genome sequence contamination as calculated by CheckV v1.1.1 against the default CheckV database v1.5. For more information about CheckV please see the tool page at: https://bitbucket.org/berkeleylab/checkv/src/master

Length of the sequence as calculated by CheckV v1.1.1 against the default CheckV database v1.5. For more information about CheckV please see the tool page at: https://bitbucket.org/berkeleylab/checkv/src/master

The number of genes in the virus sequence as calculated by CheckV v1.1.1 against the default CheckV database v1.5. For more information about CheckV please see the tool page at: https://bitbucket.org/berkeleylab/checkv/src/master

The number of host genes as calculated by CheckV v1.1.1 against the default CheckV database v1.5. For more information about CheckV please see the tool page at: https://bitbucket.org/berkeleylab/checkv/src/master

Number of times the virus genome was found in the as calculated by CheckV v1.1.1 against the default CheckV database v1.5. For more information about CheckV please see the tool page at: https://bitbucket.org/berkeleylab/checkv/src/master
MIUVIG quality as noted by CheckV v1.1.1 against the default CheckV database v1.5. For more information about CheckV please see the tool page at: https://bitbucket.org/berkeleylab/checkv/src/master

If the sequence is predicated as a provirus, the length ofthe provirus, calculated by CheckV v1.1.1 against the default CheckV database v1.5. For more information about CheckV please see the tool page at: https://bitbucket.org/berkeleylab/checkv/src/master

Prediction of whether the sequence is a provirus by CheckV v1.1.1 against the default CheckV database v1.5. [Yes/No] For more information about CheckV please see the tool page at: https://bitbucket.org/berkeleylab/checkv/src/master

Quality tier of the virus genome as noted by CheckV v1.1.1 against the default CheckV database v1.5. For more information about CheckV please see the tool page at: https://bitbucket.org/berkeleylab/checkv/src/master Possible values: Complete High-quality (>90% completeness) Medium-quality (50-90% completeness) Low-quality (<50% completeness) Undetermined quality

The number of virus genes in the virus sequence as calculated by CheckV v1.1.1 against the default CheckV database v1.5. For more information about CheckV please see the tool page at: https://bitbucket.org/berkeleylab/checkv/src/master

Warnings generated by running CheckV v1.1.1 against the default CheckV database v1.5. For more information about CheckV please see the tool page at: https://bitbucket.org/berkeleylab/checkv/src/master
City where the sample the sequence originates from was collected or sampled.
Name of the institution/affiliation to which the person collecting the sample belongs
Entry.vjdbv1.1_notes: conform with submitter organization; not sure

Entry.vjdbv1.1_description: Continent where the sample the sequence originates from was collected/sampled.
Entry.vjdbv1.1_notes: Region unclear -> Check again where it came from, recheck naming and vocabulary, INSDC country list contains the oceans; Name suggestions macroregion or georegion; no direct source but constructable (group countries into clusters);

Entry.vjdbv1.1_description: Describes the physical, environmental or local geographical source of the sample, e.g. soil
Entry.vjdbv1.1_notes: as it is, for revision with data; not sure

Entry.vjdbv1.1_description: Name of the person that collected the sample
A flag that indicates if the record is the latest update in VirJenDB database
The cuttoff used to determine if the sample is seropositive.
References an ENA assembly to an ENA Experiment.
Clear and useful title to identify the sequencing experiment. This title will not be submitted to the ENA on submission.
Genbank title of the sequence.
The level of the sequence completeness compare to the representative sequence in refseq in percentage
Virus genome sequence
VIrus genome sequence hash SHA256
Whether or not the subject is gravid. if so, report date due or date post-conception and specify which of these two dates is being reported.
Was the subject confined to a hospital as a result of virus infection or problems occurring secondary to virus infection?
Host Age with a uniform unit, TBD
Entry.vjdbv1.1_notes: Use for sorting by host age, and relevant for plotting; not sure

Entry.vjdbv1.1_description: The program used for host genome assembly.
Natural behaviour of the sampled host.

Confidence score associated with the method by which "Host Accession ID" from NCBI GenBank or "NCBI Taxonomy Lineage" was associated to the virus sequence. Multiple host associations/predictions separated by semicolons.
Other descriptive information relating to the host.
The hosts main disease diagnosis.
The hosts disease outcome. Did the host recover from the disease?

The symptoms that have been reported in relation to the illness, such as cough, diarrhea, fever, headache, malaise, myalgia, nausea, runny_nose, shortness_of_breath, sore_throat. If multiple exposures are applicable, please state them separated by semicolon.
Natural habitat of the avian or mammalian host
Health state of the host at the time of sample collection, e.g. diseased or healthy
The unique ID of the sampled host from the BV-BRC.
ICTV ID from Master_Species_List file
The list of the hash for the lineage of the rank in the ICTV VMR.
ICTV Rank Name
ICTV Rank Type
A 'Subclass' is a rank in the taxonomic hierarchy into which virus species can be classified. Defined by the ICTV.
A 'Subkingdom' is a rank in the taxonomic hierarchy into which virus species can be classified. Defined by the ICTV.
A 'Subrealm' is a rank in the taxonomic hierarchy into which virus species can be classified. Defined by the ICTV.
Commonly used virus name (or names separated by a semi-colon).
The number of days the illness lasted. example: 4
Date the subject showed an onset of symptoms. format: yyyy-mm-dd. example: 2011-10-20

The symptoms that have been reported in relation to the illness, such as cough, diarrhea, fever, headache, malaise, myalgia, nausea, runny_nose, shortness_of_breath, sore_throat. if multiple exposures are applicable, please state them separated by semicolon.
Entry.vjdb_example: fever; headache; malaise; myalgia; nausea; runny_nose

Entry.vjdbv1.1_description: IMGVR UVIG ID
Unique number of the strain which is reported as a part of the influenza strain name, such as a/chicken/fujian/411/2002(hxn1). format: integer number, example: 411
Method used to assess if a sample is flu positive/negative. Multiple methods are seperated by semicolon(s).
Result of the given methods. Positive(P) or Negative(N). For multiple test the results are delimited by semicolon, e.g. P; P.
Date that the influenza vaccination was administered to the subject over the past year. format: yyyy-mm-dd. example: 2007-05-12
Influenza vaccinations that have been administered to the subject over the last year. example: 2009 h1n1 flumist
Influenza virus type classification: A, B or C.

Entry.vjdbv1.1_name: Influenza-like Illness at the Time of Sample Collection
Entry.vjdbv1.1_description: Is the subject at the time of sample collection considered to have influenza like illness?
Dose used for the inoculoation experiment.
Brief description of the protocol inoculation route.
Is the virus stock used for the inoculation available?
Insert size for paired reads.
Date of the insertion into the VirJenDB
Instrument model used for sequencing.
Indicator that sequences came from samples from a laboratory setting, and not from a wild host.
The geographic location by latitude, where the sample the sequence originates from was collected/sampled. (DD)
The protocol used to construct the sequencing library.
The design of the sequencing library including details of how it was constructed.
The sequencing library layout specifies whether to expect single or paired configuration of reads.
The name for the sequencing library if any.
The method used to select for or against, enrich, or screen the material being sequenced.
The library source specifies the type of source material that is being sequenced.
The sequencing technique intended for this sequencing library.
Does the h1n1 influenza virus originate from a swine-like outbreak (as opposed to a seasonal flu)?
The geographic location by longitude, where the sample the sequence originates from was collected. (DD)
Description helping to explain what the cut off value means.
A 'Subclass' is a rank in the taxonomic hierarchy into which virus species can be classified. Defined by the NCBI.
A 'Subkingdom' is a rank in the taxonomic hierarchy into which virus species can be classified. Defined by the NCBI.
A 'Subrealm' is a rank in the taxonomic hierarchy into which virus species can be classified. Defined by the NCBI.
State/Region where the sample the sequence originates from was collected/sampled.
Entry.vjdbv1.1_notes: This is defined different from the duplicate below. Meaning for submission Tool was to have a geographical level higher than countries (continents). We keep it (mostly redundant), might be useful for later curation;

Entry.vjdbv1.1_description: Number of host individuals inoculated for the experiment.
The test result for other tested pathogens given as described by influenza test result.
Was the sample tested for other pathogens? Insert the pathogenic organisms using semicolon as a delimiter, e.g. Newcastle. “None” is also valid.
Pangolin version used to determine the lineage.
Taxonomy Table Hash ID of the Parent Rank
Use of personal protective equipment, such as gloves, gowns, during any type of exposure. example: mask
phage id internal identifier
Entry.vjdbv1.1_notes: From Malte's dataset, deprecated (VirJenDB Accession as new Identifier), soon to be removed

Entry.vjdbv1.1_description: The Digital Object Identifier of the publication associated with the sequence.
The PubMed ID of publication associated with the sequence.
Entry.vjdbv1.1_notes: needs curation before public; no example needed

Entry.vjdbv1.1_description: Taxonomy Table Hash ID of the Rank
If the sample was only received and not collected provide the receipt date.
RKI Accession of the virus sequence.
References a ENA assembly to an ENA Run object.
Reason for the sample collection.
Entry.vjdb_example: active surveillance in response to outbreak; market sample

Entry.vjdbv1.1_description: Free-form text describing the sample, its origin, and its method of isolation.
Temporary NCBI Tax ID used for mapping the taxonomy tree to the metadata table.
Entry.vjdbv1.1_notes: No mapping, seems to be NCBI Tax ID; not sure

Entry.vjdbv1.1_description: Conditions at which sample was stored, usually storage temperature, duration and location. in soil context: explain how and for how long the soil sample was stored before dna extraction (fresh/frozen/other).
Clear and useful title to identify the sample the sequence originates from was collected/sampled. This title will not be submitted to the ENA on submission.
Byte range data of the sample in the sequence file without the header
Generic name of the sequence (Name of the entry).
The file location of the multiseq fasta file including the parent folder name
Serological variety of a species characterised by its antigenic properties, e.g. H1N1.
Entry.vjdbv1.1_notes: NCBI Virus has genotype, which includes Serotype, otherwise we have no valid informational source -> internal for now;

Entry.vjdbv1.1_description: Designation of information related to vaccination history as self reported or documented.
Briefly describes the goals, purpose, and scope of the study. this need not be listed if it can be inherited from a referenced publication.
Clear and useful title of the study/project, e.g. a title usable for a publication or the purpose of the project and its timeframe.
Exposure of the subject to infected human or animals, such as poultry, wild bird or swine. If multiple exposures are applicable, these are separated by semicolon. Example: poultry;wild bird

Duration of the exposure of the subject to an infected human or animal. If multiple exposures are applicable, their duration is stated in the same order in which the exposure in the field was reported: 'subject exposure'. Example: 1 day; 0.33 days (Units: year)
Hash ontent for the rank in the taxonomy tree.

Setting within which the subject is exposed to animals, such as farm, slaughterhouse, food preparation. if multiple exposures are applicable, please state their type in the same order in which you reported the exposure in the field 'subject exposure'. example: backyard flock; confined animal feeding operation
Entry.vjdb_example: backyard flock; confined animal feeding operation

Entry.vjdbv1.1_description: Last update date of the sequence in BVBRC (whole entry).
Entry.vjdbv1.1_changes: update definition: last update date of the record in bvbrc)

Entry.vjdbv1.1_description: The last date the metadata was updated in the VirJen database.
List of VirJenDB metadata fields and their content when they are updated in each release in compare to the previous version
Dosage of the vaccine taken by the host. example: 0.05 ml
Lot number of the vaccine
Manufacturer of the vaccine.
Flag indicating yes if there is one page in Viralzone associated to this Taxon ID.
Unique ID given by the investigator as strain might not be unique.
Collection of outdated names for a particular virus.

Entry.vjdbv1.1_name: WHO/OIE/FAO Clade (Required for HPAI H5N1 Viruses)
Entry.vjdbv1.1_description: WHO/OIE/FAO clade should be included for highly pathogenic h5n1 viruses. example: 2.2
Percentage of Ns within the whole sequence from BVBRC.
Entry.vjdbv1.1_changes: remove; change the name and id: sequence_perc_N

Entry.vjdbv1.1_description: Number of contigs in the sequence file.
Version of the sequence (whole entry).
Why was the collected sample sequenced?
Clear and useful title to identify the analysis of your sequencing experiment. This title will not be submitted to the ENA on submission.
The level of the Assembly (contig, scaffold, chromosome, complete genome).
Entry.vjdbv1.1_notes: apply customization to vjdb , e.g. chromosome to genome or something; input not yet defined

Entry.vjdbv1.1_description: The program used for genome assembly.
Identifier on how to treat the submitted assembly. Currently only assemblies from ‘clone or isolate’ are permitted.
Entry.vjdbv1.1_notes: for submission this is always "clone or isolate";

Entry.vjdbv1.1_description: The run data file model.
The name or relative pathname of a run data file.
Host age given by a number. Is connected to Host Age Unit. The number should match the unit. E.g. 2 years
Unit that describes the host age. Is connected to Host Age. Should fit the number, e.g. 2 years
Entry.vjdbv1.1_notes: These two private for submission, one field for public where it is combined and one for normalized values (also for filtering);

Entry.vjdbv1.1_description: BVBRC Lab Host, common name of the lab host.
Entry.vjdbv1.1_notes: remove? Lab Passage 1 to 1 in public and rest in private for submission, because Lab Passaged is buil on "/labhost", to catch this we provide these fields; not sure

Entry.vjdbv1.1_description: The NCBI Taxonomy ID of the lab host.
The scientific species name of the lab host.
Minimum length of consecutive Ns to be considered a gap.
NCBI Assembly Accession of the virus sequence.
Average sequencing depth across the sequence from BVBRC.
References the BioProject Accession that the virus sequence is a part of (INSDC).
References the BioSample Accession that the virus sequence is a part of (INSDC).
Accession of the sequence in the BV-BRC database.
Entry.vjdbv1.1_changes: Change id and name to BVBRC_genome_id

Entry.vjdbv1.1_description: Host Age with unit from BV-BRC.
Entry.vjdbv1.1_notes: Seperation of values good for submission, display should be in one, needs curation from ENA values, seperate Fields for submission?

Entry.vjdbv1.1_description: The host's broader group association in a taxonomic context from BV-BRC.
Sex of the sampled host from the BV-BRC.
Flag indicating if this virus sequence is available in BV-BRC.
The VirJenDB ID of the representative of its cluster. See documentation for selection details.
Flag indicating yes if the sequence is a representative sequence of a group of sequences, computed by the VirJenDB clustering workflow. See documentation for selection details.
True if the current virus sequence is a coding strand. Calculated by part of the ViralClust tool suite. Only intended for ssRNA viruses.
Date when the sample the virus sequence originates from was collected or sampled.
Entry.vjdbv1.1_notes: would be "Date" but to make distinction as there are multiple dates;

Entry.vjdbv1.1_description: Country name and additional identifier like a state abbreviation from where the sample the sequence originates from was collected/sampled.
Tissue, Specimen or Source of the superhost from which the sample the virus sequence originates from was collected or sampled.
Entry.vjdbv1.1_notes: Plan about sources and vocabularies, RKI different from ENA, what about NCBI and environmental, leave it like that for now with NCBi input;

Entry.vjdbv1.1_description: Country or sea area where the sample the virus sequence originates from was collected or sampled.
Percentage of G and C nucleotides in the sequence from BVBRC.
True if this virus sequence is available in Genbank.
Whether the sequence is a partial or complete sequence in NCBI GenBank.

The genotype or subtype of a virus sequence, as provided by the sequence submitter. This field comes from the "/serotype" field of the GenBank record and is shown as submitted. Consistency and accuracy may vary.
Entry.vjdbv1.1_notes: Is sample needed at all? Do we relate everything to the sequence/the virus -> Variant, Taxonomy Decision: Variant or NCBI/ICTV Variant; no example needed

Entry.vjdbv1.1_description: Host Accession from NCBI GenBank that refers to the host sequence from which the prophage was predicted.
Host age number from "BVBRC Host Age". Caution: not yet standardized by unit!
Entry.vjdbv1.1_notes: Seperation of values good for submission, display should be in one, needs curation from ENA values, seperate Fields for submission?;

Entry.vjdbv1.1_description: Method by which the fields "Host Accession ID" from NCBI GenBank or "NCBI Taxonomy Lineage" was associated to the virus sequence. Currently (v1.1) either a host association from the data source or PhiSpy prophage prediction. Multiple host associations/predictions separated by semicolons.
Average sequencing depth across the host sequence. Multiple host associations/predictions separated by semicolons.
References the BioProject Accession associated with the host sequence.
References the BioSample Accession associated with the host sequence.
Date of collection of the sample from which the host sequence originates.
Common name of the host the virus sequence was collected from, e.g. human. Currently (v1.1) no ontology is applied.
Entry.vjdbv1.1_tags: ["ENA", "NCBI Virus", "BV-BRC", "ENA ERC32", "ENA ERC33", "ENA Sample", "VJDB Sample", "Host", "Host Taxonomy"]

Entry.vjdbv1.1_description: Country or sea area origin of the sample from which the host sequence originates.
References the Host GTDB Species Name to which the NCBI host taxonomy ID of the virus sequence could be mapped. See the mapping file on the Datasets page.
Associated Host IMGM Taxon OID from which the virus sequence was extracted by IMG/VR.
The host sequence NCBI Taxonomy ID. Can be based on the mapping by GTDB of the GTDB Species Name to the NCBI Taxonomy ID.
Instrument or sequencing platform used for host sequencing. Multiple host associations/predictions separated by semicolons.
The scientific species name of the sampled host.
A 'Class' is a rank in the taxonomic hierarchy into which a virus sequence can be classified. Defined by the ICTV.
Flag indicating yes if the sequence is an exemplar of the species in the ICTV Taxonomy.
A 'Family' is a rank in the taxonomic hierarchy into which a virus sequence can be classified. Defined by the ICTV.
A 'Genus' is a rank in the taxonomic hierarchy into which a virus sequence can be classified. Defined by the ICTV.
The ICTV Host source field.
A 'Kingdom' is a rank in the taxonomic hierarchy into which a virus sequence can be classified. Defined by the ICTV.
Aggregation of the ICTV Taxonomy Names associated with the virus sequence.
A 'Order' is a rank in the taxonomic hierarchy into which a virus sequence can be classified. Defined by the ICTV.
A 'Phylum' is a rank in the taxonomic hierarchy into which a virus sequence can be classified. Defined by the ICTV.
A 'Realm' is the highest taxonomic rank into which a virus sequence can be classified. Defined by the ICTV.

A Species is the lowest taxonomic rank in the hierarchy approved by the ICTV. While subspecies levels of classification may exist for some viruses (e.g. Hepatitis C virus), the ICTV does not classify viruses below the species level. Defined by the ICTV.
A 'Subfamily' is a rank in the taxonomic hierarchy into which a virus sequence can be classified. Defined by the ICTV.
A 'Subgenus' is a rank in the taxonomic hierarchy into which a virus sequence can be classified. Defined by the ICTV.
A 'suborder' is a rank in the taxonomic hierarchy into which a virus sequence can be classified. Defined by the ICTV.
A 'Subphylum' is a rank in the taxonomic hierarchy into which a virus sequence can be classified. Defined by the ICTV.
GenBank Accession of the virus sequence. It equals NCBI Nucleotide Accession, ENA Accession, and DDBJ Accession, which are part of the INSDC.
Name of the virus isolate associated with the sample from which the virus sequence was obtained.
Entry.vjdbv1.1_tags: ["ENA", "NCBI Virus", "ENA ERC32", "ENA ERC33", "ENA Sample", "VJDB Sample", "Sample", "Virus Info"]

Entry.vjdbv1.1_description: Molecule type of the sequence, e.g. ssRNA. Note that there are 15 molecule types corresponding to combinations of the Baltimore Classifications, from NCBI GenBank.
Entry.vjdbv1.1_notes: Should be curated into Baltimore classes; Is this a sequence attribute or sample or both? Represent in naming?;

Entry.vjdbv1.1_description: Sub-species designation of the virus sequence, from NCBI GenBank field "clade".
A 'Class' is a rank in the taxonomic hierarchy into which a virus sequence can be classified. Defined by the NCBI.
A 'Family' is a rank in the taxonomic hierarchy into which a virus sequence can be classified. Defined by the NCBI.
Sub-species designation of the virus sequence, from NCBI GenBank field "genotype".
A 'Genus' is a rank in the taxonomic hierarchy into which virus a sequence can be classified. Defined by the NCBI.
Sub-species designation of the virus sequence, from NCBI GenBank field "isolate".
A 'Kingdom' is a rank in the taxonomic hierarchy into which a virus sequence can be classified. Defined by the NCBI.
NCBI TaxIDs assigned to the virus sequence, from BVBRC.
Entry.vjdbv1.1_changes: add|we will add NCBI Tax Names from the NCBI Taxonomy DB later

Entry.vjdbv1.1_description: Aggregation of the NCBI Taxonomy Names associated with the virus sequence.
A 'Order' is a rank in the taxonomic hierarchy into which a virus sequence can be classified. Defined by the NCBI.
A 'Phylum' is a rank in the taxonomic hierarchy into which a virus sequence can be classified. Defined by the NCBI.
A 'Realm' is the highest taxonomic rank into which virus species can be classified. Defined by the NCBI.
Sub-species designation of the virus sequence, from NCBI GenBank field "serotype".

A Species is the lowest taxonomic rank in the hierarchy approved by the NCBI. While subspecies levels of classification may exist for some viruses (e.g. Hepatitis C virus), the NCBI does not classify viruses below the species level. Defined by the NCBI.
Entry.vjdbv1.1_notes: NCBI Virus has only the virus species in this field, Taxonomy Descision; no example needed

Entry.vjdbv1.1_description: The virus species NCBI Taxonomy ID.
A 'Subfamily' is a rank in the taxonomic hierarchy into which a virus sequence can be classified. Defined by the NCBI.
A 'Subgenus' is a rank in the taxonomic hierarchy into which a virus sequence can be classified. Defined by the NCBI.
A 'suborder' is a rank in the taxonomic hierarchy into which a virus sequence can be classified. Defined by the NCBI.
A 'Subphylum' is a rank in the taxonomic hierarchy into which a virus sequence can be classified. Defined by the NCBI.
NCBI GenBank organism name: a taxonomic name at species level or below the species level.
Entry.vjdbv1.1_notes: technically sample species or below, take as it is until curation;

Entry.vjdbv1.1_description: Lineage as determined by Pangolin.
Entry.vjdbv1.1_notes: depends on how to treat RKI data, they have pangolin leange in json format that includes version and also method information, we could either include that (bad readabiliy, or splt the relevant information for these fields)

Entry.vjdbv1.1_description: Instrument platform used for sequencing the virus; multiple values are seperated by a semicolon (only for assemblies).
The tool used to predict the virus sequence. No value means that it is not a predicted virus sequence.
Determines if this virus sequence is a reference sequence in the RefSeq database.
Date when the sequence was made public in a repository (e.g. GenBank, BV-BRC).
Name or number of the virus segment.
Length of the virus sequence in basepairs (bp).
Entry.vjdbv1.1_notes: Is sequence in the name needed? Length to generic -> Sequence Length (consideration for other data layers);

Entry.vjdbv1.1_description: The name of the source database or tool by which the virus sequence was added in VirJenDB; in the case of PhiSpy-predicted prophages, the value is PhiSpy.
Entry.vjdbv1.1_changes: change id and name to source_metadata_db, update definition

Entry.vjdbv1.1_description: Institution that sequenced the collected sample.
NCBI Sequence Read Archive (SRA) Accession.
Name of the virus strain associated with the sample from which the virus sequence was obtained.
Name(s) of the submitter(s) of the virus sequence.
Country of the submitter's organization.
Organization the submitter(s) is(are) affiliated with.
Entry.vjdb_example: National Center for Biotechnology Information, NIH

Entry.vjdbv1.1_description: Region or location of the organization of the submitters.
The VirJenDB ID for the unique representative sequence. For the non-phages, the smallest Accession in the group. See documentation for selection details.
Flag indicating yes if the sequence is a representative for a group of identical VirJenDB sequences.
Unique identifier of the virus sequence, assigned by VirJenDB.
Version of the virus sequence record, including metadata, assigned by VirJenDB.
URL to the GenBank record from the ICTV VMR
